findings.source: Bargh et al. (1996) — Journal of Personality and Social Psychology
findings.claim: Elderly priming significantly reduced walking speed in participants (p < 0.05), demonstrating automatic behavior priming effects.
Only 36% of psychology studies replicated successfully. Effect sizes in replications were on average half those of originals.
findings.contradiction: Systemic replication failure undermines the foundational confidence in priming studies and similar behavioral experiments.

findings.claim: Direct replication of Bargh's elderly priming study found no effect when experimenter was blinded. Results suggest original findings were driven by experimenter expectancy bias.
findings.contradiction: Failed direct replication. Identifies a critical methodological flaw (lack of blinding) in the original study.

findings.claim: Priming effects may be real but highly context-dependent, requiring specific moderating conditions not captured in simplified lab paradigms.
findings.consensus: Attempts to reconcile conflicting evidence through boundary condition analysis.

forensicConclusion: SIGNIFICANT METHODOLOGICAL CONFLICT. The foundational priming study (Bargh 1996) fails direct blinded replication (Doyen 2012). The broader reproducibility audit (OSC 2015) confirms systemic issues. Cesario's reconciliation suggests context-dependency, but the original claims of universal automatic priming effects are not supported by the replication evidence.
